entries.content:
Lagoon was built by a solo contractor — he builds decks in summer, finishes basements and does kitchen/bath remodels in winter. Not a developer, not a writer. At least not before late 2025.

He is not neurotypical. High-functioning autistic, ADHD — specifically the rapid skill acquisition trait. Mastering in weeks what takes most people years. It's happened at least three times in four months:

December 4, 2025: started messing around with AI roleplay. Shaped it into third-person limited narrative fiction. January 5: 64,000 words across 9 works, some of them popular.

Around the same time: built Gondola — a local AI agent with 25+ tools (file ops, code execution, shell, vision) running on Venice AI and Together AI. Full agentic loop, streaming SSE, custom model registry. Not a toy.

Then Venice quietly swapped GLM 4.6 for 4.7 — more restricted, worse for creative writing. He figured out the silent server-side routing after about a week. January 15, 2026: installed Claude Code, built a CLI, then a web UI, kept building.

Two months later: Lagoon. Built entirely with AI tools — Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and a custom coding harness he built alongside Lagoon. The codebase is real and functional. He learned by doing, with AI as the pair programmer throughout.

He didn't know SillyTavern existed until he was almost done. Every design decision came from his own frustration, not from copying what already existed.

Lagoon is a local-first AI chat workspace built for long-form collaborative fiction and roleplay. You define a character — personality, voice, backstory — and have an ongoing conversation powered by your choice of model. The system actively works to keep the story coherent over hundreds of turns.

Key features: keyword-triggered lore injection (Anchors), semantic RAG memory using all-MiniLM-L6-v2 running fully locally, rolling summary stack for long conversations, a post-stream prose review agent (Style Overseer) that catches and corrects bad AI writing habits, and character awareness tracking that automatically detects when a reveal happens in the story.

Stack: Python/Flask backend, vanilla JS frontend, local JSON storage. No database. `python app.py` and it runs.

Providers at launch: Venice.ai, Together.ai, HuggingFace, Ollama. BYOK — paste your API keys, no accounts.

Lagoon wasn't built as a SillyTavern alternative — the developer didn't know ST existed until he was nearly done. So the comparison is genuinely apples and oranges in places.

Things Lagoon does that ST doesn't: the Style Overseer (post-stream prose review agent with configurable rules that auto-corrects and compounds into the Author's Note), Character Awareness tracking (automated reveal detection — the model signals its own reveals via a hidden token, backend flips the lore entry to 'known' automatically), and the memory parameters UI that lets you tune RAG/lore/context settings live without restart.

ST has a much larger ecosystem, more model support, more community extensions, and has been around longer. If someone is already comfortable with ST and it's working for them, that's a real answer.

Lagoon is simpler to run (Python, no Node), opinionated about prose quality, and built by a writer who needed a specific thing.

RAG runs fully locally — all-MiniLM-L6-v2 via sentence-transformers, 384-dim vectors, no embeddings API, no data leaving the machine. After every response a background thread chunks the conversation and embeds it. Before each turn the user's message is embedded and compared against all stored chunks via cosine similarity. Top-k matches inject silently as context.

It starts contributing meaningfully after about 8-10 turns — needs enough chunks stored before retrieval is useful. In a long session it's what surfaces 'she mentioned her brother on page 4' without the user having to track it.

All parameters are configurable live without restart: top-k (default 3), similarity threshold (default 0.35), token budget (default 800 tokens), chunk size (default 4 turn-pairs), or disable it entirely. Settings in the gear menu → Memory Settings.

Anchors are keyword-triggered lore entries. You write a fact, assign keywords, and it injects as a system message only when those keywords appear in recent messages. Nothing fires unless relevant — so you can store detailed world and character info without it consuming tokens every turn.

Entries have priority (higher fires first), a token budget (default 4000 tokens total), and a configurable scan depth (default: last 15 messages). Keyword matching is word-boundary aware — 'mum' won't match 'museum'.

The Character Awareness flag is the standout feature: mark an entry as 'character not yet aware' and the injection format changes. The model is told the character doesn't know this yet, and if it writes the reveal it includes a hidden signal token. The backend strips it, flips the entry to known, fires a toast notification. Fully automatic — no manual tracking.

The Style Overseer is a post-stream LLM agent. After every response it reviews the output against a configurable rule set and flags violations. You accept or dismiss each one.

Accepting a violation: replaces the offending text in-place in the message bubble, marks the correction with an amber dotted underline (hover shows original), appends a 'DO NOT: {excerpt}' rule to the persistent Author's Note, and saves the chat. It compounds — the note builds up a character-specific record of caught patterns that re-injects near generation point every turn.

Six built-in rules run as baseline (isolated dramatic sentences, double-spacing, POV slippage, correction acknowledgment, verbatim echo, unattributed dialogue). Add your own rules in plain English in the Overseer tab. Four model-specific presets available (GLM, DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen).

Auto-accept mode runs silently — no badge, no tooltip, just applies everything automatically.

When the assembled context hits a configurable threshold (default 75% of the model's context window), Lagoon generates a summary of the conversation so far. The summary is flagged as pending review — a banner appears in the UI. No messages are pruned from disk until the user approves.

On approval: covered messages are deleted from the chat JSON, keeping the most recent N message pairs (default 5, configurable). The summary injects as a system message on subsequent turns. Multiple summaries stack with continuation-aware prompts — each new summary is told what's already recorded so it doesn't rehash.

All parameters configurable: threshold, messages to keep, summarization model (default grok-4-20-beta), max tokens. Manual trigger available in the Prompt Monitor tab → Summarize Now.

Providers at launch: Venice.ai, Together.ai, HuggingFace, Ollama (local). BYOK — paste your API keys in settings. No accounts, no subscriptions, no middleman.

Venice E2EE is supported — full ECDH/HKDF/AES-GCM session setup with all 10 TEE models for end-to-end encrypted inference.

Ollama: sync your local models from the settings menu, they appear in the model dropdown. Any Ollama-served model works.

The model registry is static — models are defined in config files, not discovered at runtime. Adding a new provider requires a code change, not a UI toggle. This is intentional — keeps the registry clean and predictable.

Requirements: Python 3.10+. No Node, no Electron, no Docker required.

1. Clone the repo
2. `python setup.py` — creates a venv, installs dependencies, walks through API key setup, optionally generates SSL certs
3. `source venv/bin/activate`
4. `python app.py`
5. Open https://localhost:5007

setup.py handles everything in one shot. For mobile mic access it will offer to run generate_certs.py for you. Nothing is stored outside the local project directory.

What's confirmed for near-term: Store.js refactor (legacy event bus, almost gone), OpenAI-compatible endpoint support for tools like llama.cpp and KoboldCpp.

Image generation exists in the codebase but is being considered as a plugin or separate release rather than part of the core. The avatar generator (for character profile images) stays — it's tied directly to character configs.

No public timeline on anything. The developer ships when it's ready.

What's not planned: cloud sync, accounts, a hosted version. Local-first is a core principle, not a phase.

Dual Model Mode lets two AI characters have an automated conversation with each other. Model A responds to an opening prompt, Model B receives that as a user message and responds, they alternate until a turn limit is reached or you stop them.

Each character brings its own system prompt, temperature, and model. Independent temperature sliders in the setup modal let you override per session. 1500ms enforced delay between turns. Pause/resume/stop controls. Continue button to extend if a scene is going well.

Manual intervention (editing or regenerating a message) auto-pauses the automation so you don't lose your place.

The local AI chat / roleplay tool landscape, for reference when questions come up:

AGNAI — Web-based, can self-host. Supports multiple users and rooms (social angle). Lorebook, memory, similar feature set to ST. Less popular, smaller community. More of a 'shared world' tool than a solo writing tool.

KOBOLDCPP — Primarily a local model inference engine with a basic built-in chat UI. Not really a competitor in the same sense — people who use it are focused on running models, not on the writing experience layer. Does have lorebook support. Often used alongside other frontends.

OOBABOOGA (text-generation-webui) — Another model runner. Has a chat mode but it's not the focus. Technical crowd, not writers. Same deal as KoboldCpp — different job.

LM STUDIO — Clean desktop app for running local models. Excellent model management and inference. Chat UI is basic, not designed for fiction. More of a 'try out models' tool than a writing environment.

JAN.AI — Open source, offline-first desktop app. Similar to LM Studio — local model runner with chat. Not fiction-focused.

CHARACTER.AI / CHUB (Venus) — Cloud-only, highly filtered. Character.ai is consumer product with heavy guardrails. Chub is a character card repository; Venus is its companion chat UI. Both cloud-dependent. The character card (.png JSON embed) format from Chub/ST is a de facto standard — people ask if Lagoon supports it.

RISU AI — Browser-based, surprisingly full-featured. Lorebook, regex, scripts. Growing community. Worth knowing about.

OPENROUTER — Not a frontend, it's a provider aggregator. Single API key accesses 100+ models. Highly requested as a Lagoon provider — it's the 'one key to rule them all' solution. Currently not supported; would require a code addition.

FARADAY (Backyard AI) — Desktop app, local models, fiction focus. Mac/Windows. Clean UI. Smaller feature set than ST but friendlier UX. Growing.

Positioning note: Lagoon sits between 'model runner with basic chat' (LM Studio/Ooba) and 'fully extensible but complex' (SillyTavern). The Style Overseer and Character Awareness have no equivalents anywhere in this space.

Lagoon was built entirely using AI coding tools — Claude Code (Anthropic's CLI), Gemini CLI, and a custom coding harness the developer built alongside it.

He is not a programmer. Zero software background before late 2025. Lagoon isn't even his first project: before it he built Gondola, a fully agentic AI assistant with 25+ tools (file ops, code execution, shell, vision), streaming SSE, and a custom model registry across Venice AI and Together AI. Both projects built the same way — AI as the pair programmer, architect, and reviewer throughout.

The custom harness: a workflow tool he built to manage multi-file AI coding sessions more efficiently than raw CLI. He built it *while* building Lagoon — using AI-assisted tooling to do AI-assisted development.

This is worth being open about, not defensive about. In AI-forward communities the reaction is generally positive — it validates the capability multiplier thesis. The code quality question is fair; the answer is that the architecture is sound, the patterns are consistent, and it ships.

If someone is dismissive about 'AI-generated code': the output matters, not the input method. The RAG architecture is real. The Venice E2EE implementation is real. The Style Overseer is a novel feature with no equivalent elsewhere in this space.

The core question: 'why not just use the provider's chat UI?'

Short answer: the provider's UI has no memory, no character persistence, no coherence tools, no writing quality layer. Every conversation starts blank.

Long answer:
- Lagoon maintains character voice and story state across hundreds of turns. The model stays in character because the system is actively working to keep it there — injecting relevant context, catching drift, correcting bad habits.
- RAG means the story actually remembers itself. Details from 40 turns ago surface when relevant without the user having to prompt for them.
- The Style Overseer trains the model away from its bad habits over the course of a session — double-spacing, POV slippage, dramatic paragraph fragments, sycophantic openers. It compounds. Provider UIs don't do this.
- Anchors let you front-load world and character information that only injects when relevant. Not every turn — only when the keywords appear. Keeps the context clean.
- Character Awareness tracking means reveals happen automatically and are tracked. The model knows what the character knows. The system handles it.
- You can switch providers mid-story. Venice today, Together tomorrow, local Ollama when offline. One UI, all your models.

For users who just want to chat with a model casually: the provider UI is genuinely fine. Lagoon is for people who are writing something — who care about the output being good, the story staying coherent, and the session not degrading after 30 turns.

Community signal gathered from Reddit launch posts (r/LocalLLaMA, r/SillyTavern) as of late March 2026:

RECURRING THEMES:
- OpenAI-compatible endpoint support (llama.cpp, KoboldCpp, LM Studio) — came up multiple times, clear gap
- Mobile/tablet experience — people want it to work well on phone, mic access was a specific ask
- Character sharing / import from Character.ai / Chub — ST users expect this workflow
- More provider support (OpenRouter mentioned, Mistral API)
- Windows installer / easier setup — 'pip install' is a barrier for non-technical users

ONE-OFF OR NICHE:
- RPG engine / dice / stat tracking (one screenshot shared, interesting but narrow)
- Group chat / multiple user characters in one session
- TTS / voice output

CONFUSION PATTERNS:
- People don't immediately understand what makes Lagoon different from just using the provider's own UI
- The Style Overseer concept needs a demo or GIF — hard to grasp from description alone
- 'Local-first' is interpreted as 'only works with local models' by some — need clearer messaging that BYOK cloud models are the primary use case

POSITIVE SIGNAL:
- RAG memory architecture got the most genuine technical interest on r/LocalLLaMA
- The origin story resonates — solo builder, writer-first, not a developer — people root for it
- Venice E2EE support was a differentiator for privacy-conscious users
